title: Student Success Center
summary: Student Success Center coaching is for the messy middle: routines, overwhelm, getting organized, and finding a support plan before everything snowballs.

modes.label: In person
modes.summary: Use the Student Success Center for face-to-face coaching about routines, stress points, and staying on track.

steps.title: Scan coaching options first
steps.description: The center often offers multiple ways in: coaching appointments, events, and pop-up support.
bullets: Open success.asu.edu and look for coaching or support options.
Pick the format that feels easiest to try first.
Save the time and location if you are going in person.

visual.title: Coaching, events, and support touchpoints
visual.caption: This is often the best first stop when the problem is “everything feels scattered.”

steps.title: Show up with the real version of the problem
steps.description: This works best when you name the actual pattern: missed deadlines, stress spirals, not knowing where to start, or difficulty sticking to routines.
bullets: Bring your calendar, current to-do list, or MyASU tasks if you have them.
Start with the one habit or stress point that keeps repeating.
Ask what support would make the next week feel more manageable.
steps.reassurance: You do not need a dramatic crisis to use this. Many students go because they are quietly struggling to stay on top of normal college life.

visual.title: Calendar, tasks, and the real problem pattern
visual.caption: The coach can help more when they can see how your week actually feels.

steps.title: Leave with a small system, not a pep talk
steps.description: A strong coaching visit gives you one new routine, tool, or follow-up support path to try right away.
bullets: Ask for one realistic routine change you can test this week.
Ask whether another resource should be part of your plan too.
Write the next action before you leave.

visual.title: A small system you can actually use
visual.caption: Useful support feels like something you can repeat tomorrow, not just inspiration in the moment.

modes.label: Online
modes.summary: Use online coaching or virtual support when you want the same planning help without traveling to a campus office.

steps.title: Find the online coaching option
steps.description: Use the Student Success Center site to find the virtual appointment, workshop, or event that fits your need.
bullets: Open success.asu.edu and look for virtual coaching or events.
Choose the option that feels easiest to commit to.
Save the meeting link or event instructions.

visual.title: Online coaching without the extra friction
visual.caption: Sometimes removing the commute is enough to make support feel doable.

steps.title: Bring your real week to the call
steps.description: Virtual coaching works best when you have your actual schedule and tasks visible, not just a vague sense of stress.
bullets: Open your calendar, course list, or task app before the meeting.
Know which part of the week keeps breaking down.
Be ready to say whether the issue is starting tasks, keeping routines, or missing deadlines.
steps.reassurance: It is okay if your answer is basically “my week feels messy and I do not know why.” That is a real coaching starting point.

visual.title: Let the coach see the shape of your week
visual.caption: Specific schedules usually reveal the problem faster than abstract stress does.

steps.title: Pick one change you will actually try
steps.description: The fastest way to waste coaching is to leave with too many ideas. One repeatable change is better.
bullets: Choose one tool, habit, or support follow-up to try first.
Ask how to tell whether the change is actually helping.
Write down when you will test it.

visual.title: One change with a real test window
visual.caption: Good coaching is practical enough to show up in your next three days.

modes.label: Drop-in
modes.summary: Use pop-up coaching or a lower-pressure drop-in option when booking a full appointment feels like too much friction.

steps.title: Find the easiest support doorway
steps.description: The Student Success Center often has lower-pressure events or quick support touchpoints that do not feel like a formal appointment.
bullets: Check the site for pop-up coaching, workshops, or same-day support.
Pick the easiest format to try this week.
Save the location or time before you close the page.

visual.title: Use the doorway that feels easiest to walk through
visual.caption: Sometimes the best support is the one with the least friction.

steps.title: State the one pattern that keeps repeating
steps.description: For a short conversation, naming the repeat problem clearly is more useful than telling the whole semester story.
bullets: Try: “I keep falling behind because I never know what to start first.”
Or: “I am going to class, but everything else feels disorganized.”
Ask what one tool or support would help most first.
steps.reassurance: This is exactly the kind of support that exists for students who are not in crisis but are also not okay doing it all alone.

visual.title: Name the repeating pattern, not the whole biography
visual.caption: That usually gets you to the useful advice faster.

steps.title: Turn the quick help into a follow-up plan
steps.description: If the short conversation helps, lock in the next step while you still have momentum.
bullets: Ask whether a coaching appointment, advising visit, or another resource should come next.
Write down the next step and when you will do it.
If they suggest another office, open that link before the day ends.

visual.title: Quick support should connect to the next real move
visual.caption: A short conversation is useful when it points you somewhere concrete.

questionSet.defaultOpener: Hi, I think I need help getting more organized and I want to know what kind of support would actually help.
questionSet.defaultProTip: Bring your real calendar or task list if you can. Coaching gets more practical when the week is visible.
questionSet.firstTimeIntro: This is a good first-stop resource when the problem feels too spread out to explain neatly.

scenarioPlaceholders.class: I am keeping up in some classes, but my routines are not strong enough and I feel like I am slipping.
scenarioPlaceholders.money: Stress about money is making it harder to stay organized and keep up with everything.
scenarioPlaceholders.overwhelmed: I feel overwhelmed by all the different things I am supposed to keep track of.
scenarioPlaceholders.schedule: My week feels disorganized and I need help building a system that actually works.
scenarioPlaceholders.something-else: I know I need support with routines and staying on top of things, but I do not know what to ask for.

first-year: What kind of support do first-year students usually get from the Student Success Center?
What is one routine I should build now before things get more complicated?
How do I figure out which support resource should be part of my plan next?
What should I do if I feel behind but I cannot even tell where to start?
What is one question students usually do not realize they can ask here?
second-year: What system would help me stay more consistent this semester?
How do I know whether my problem is organization, workload, or both?
What is one support step I can add this week without making life more complicated?
How do I build a routine that actually lasts past the first few days?
What other ASU resource should I pair with this if needed?
third-year: What should I focus on first if my workload and responsibilities both feel heavy?
How can I build a realistic system instead of an ideal one I will not keep?
What signs suggest I need another resource in addition to coaching?
How do I make sure this support turns into an actual weekly change?
What is the smartest next step from here?
fourth-year-plus: What kind of structure helps most when the stakes are higher later in college?
How do I keep small problems from turning into graduation-timeline problems?
What should I prioritize first if my week feels overloaded?
How do I build a plan that is realistic for this stage of school?
What is one question students at my stage forget to ask?
